headline: The Hum Mystery: Scientists Point to the Ears Themselves
meta_description: Decades-old mystery of unexplained humming heard worldwide may originate inside the ear itself, not from external sources.
summary_short: For decades, 2-4% of people worldwide have reported hearing an unexplained hum. Norwegian researchers now suggest it may be low-frequency tinnitus.
body:
For nearly fifty years, people across the globe have reported hearing a mysterious low-frequency hum with no identifiable source. What began in Bristol, England during the mid-1970s has since spread to cities from Taos, New Mexico to Oslo, Norway. Some hear it as a manageable buzz; others experience physical discomfort or vibrations through their body.

Now, researchers at the Norwegian University of Science and Technology may have found the answer—and it's surprisingly intimate. Rather than coming from distant machines or natural phenomena, the hum might originate within the auditory system itself.

Professor Markus Drexl and his team studied 28 people in Germany who reported hearing the unexplained sound. They tested two hypotheses: whether the hum was an externally produced noise, or whether those who hear it possess exceptional sensitivity to low-frequency sounds.

The results were revealing. Most participants showed no unusual hearing ability. Instead, researchers found the strongest evidence pointing to low-frequency tinnitus—sounds the inner ear produces naturally, which most people never consciously perceive.

This explanation offers genuine hope. Understanding that the hum originates within, rather than from an elusive external source, opens new possibilities for those struggling with the phenomenon. Sometimes, the mystery we search for everywhere around us has been with us all along.
